CURRENT AFFAIRS,

The extraordinary general meeting of the Great
3 Southern and Western Railway was held on Monday
~ Jast. Sir William looked glum; and when Mr. Sexton
se to speak, Mr. William Murphy placed his left hand
his left ear—the Independent was evidently anxious
hear what the Freeman was about to say. Tho meet-

comparatively a small one, and at no time was
ere anything like excitement displayed. The jaunty
air that one is accustomed to associate with the Baronet
" of the Board was to seek; and when Sir William spoke
he did so almost with tears in his voice, if we may put
it so. Frederick 0. Pilkington, J.P., D.L., one of the
~ financial giants of the National Assurance Company,
discreetly said nothing. The directors do not want an
v nquiry, and they used their proxies to stifle it. That

tﬁ is the situation which the shareholders now have to

28 face. Is that a situation calculated to create con-
e dence in the Board, or is it calculated to engender un-

The speech of the day was undoubtedly that of Mr.
Sexton. But it was a battle not of reason and debate,
but of proxies. Sir William and the directors gathered
in the proxies by an appeal partly to partisanship and
religious bigotry. It was plain from the speeches and
from many of the proxi_s sent to Mr. Sullivan that no
such complexion could be placed on a purely business
endeavour to procure an inquiry into the affairs of the
Company ; yet Sir Willian threw the weight of the
proxies: into the baulk-inquiry scale.

Several attempts were made to drag religious de-
nominationalism into this purely business matter, parti-
cularly by a gentleman whose wit is by no means as
keen as is his painful desire to appear funny—one Dr.
Faulkiner, K.C. As a matter of fact, in recent years
there was no attempt on the part of any body of share-
holders to drag religious denominationalism into the
business of the Company; what the shareholders re-
ferred to really did was to protest against the manage-
ment having allowed religious denominational in-
fluence to affect the staffing of the Railway. It was
the management that introduced, or permitted the in-
troduction of, religious denominational influence ; what
some of the shareholders did, and this paper lent them
at least a helping hand in the matter, was to protest
against the operation of such influences, and to claim
Justice, Toleration, and Fair Play for all. The com-
petitive examination for clerkships, that resulted from
the shareholders protest against sectarian bias being
dragged into business, shows that the protest bore con-
siderable fruit; and that fruit may stand as the justifi-
cation of the protest. All that is ancient history now ;
but it is well to bear in mind that the action of some
shareholders at that time was a protest against the
existence of sectarian influénce in the business of the
Company.

The attempt was made to arouse sectarian animosity
of a certain well-known virulent type on the side of
the directors (we do not know if the man Coote, of
Tyrone, Justice of the Peace, is a sharecholder) and
against those who sought for a business inquiry into
the affairs of a depreciated Company, one of whose
directors is also one of the financial giants under whose
pressure the assets of the National Assurance Company
have not only vanished, but have been turned into a
heavy minus quantity—whatever success that unworthy
attempt may have met with, the baulk-inquiry party
have succeeded ; and the full weight of the proxies were
thrown i1 on the side of Sir William Goulding and his
co-directors.

Mr. Sweetman remarked:—‘‘ They were told of
course they should have implicit confidence in the direc-
tors (a laugh). But they should remember the ex-
perience of the National Assurance Company of Ire-
land, and when they would remember that, they would
come to the conclusion that that doctrine was played
out.”’

We quote the following, full of pith, from the speech
of Mr. Sexton:—‘“ A few yecars ago you had a system
<f 671 miles of railway bringing in an average receipt
per year of £1,400 a mile, the working expenses were
£800 a mile, the profit was therefore £600 a mile, and
out of that profit you paid, and you easily paid, divi-
dends of from b to 5} per cent. upon your ordinary
stock. How came the happy scene to change? The
amalgamations arrived. I hear it said that the amal-
gamations were unavoidable if you were to escape from
ruin. That is rather a matter of speculative opinion.
if the argument could be made good it would, no doubt,

be very material, because to escape from ruin people do · 
and suffer much. But the question of the terms re-
mains (hear, hear). 
You bought 400 miles of railway, 
and the result of the operation was that the receipts 
per mile for the whole expanded system were down 
from £1,400 to £1,260 a mile; that is, they we1·e clown

Of

,charges of the debenture class remained about the same 
:2s. 3d.; the preference • charges were also about th~

The downward course 
had begun. 
Last year and this may explain why stock •

Last year of every £1 of traffic receipts earned

,and the bal_ance left for ordinary dividend out of that 
£1 of traffic receipts which had been 5s. lld. in 1897, 
_:_and 4s. 7d. in 1900, fell down to 3s. ld."

The Drunkenness (Ireland) Bill is going t hrough its 
stages in Parliament. 
Clause 1 provides that where, in 
the opinion of a court of summary jurisdiction, a mar-
ried man is a habitual drunkard, as defined by Section 
3 of the Habitual Drunkards Act, 1879, the court may, 
on the application of any person, make an order under 
this Act " protecting certain specified earnings and pro-
perty appertaining to the wife, her children, or step-
children," including "any furniture, bedding, or other 
articles in use as household necessaries in her residence ." 
The bill-somewhat amended from its original form-
got through the Grand Committee and was ordered to 
be reported to the House.

We note that the May meeting of the Executive of
the Gaelic League is over, and that the resolution at
the previous meeting ostentatiously refusing to condemn
street blackguardism committed in the name of the
League was not rescinded. We have publicly asked for
particulars of the voting, etc., concerning that resolu-
tion, but no further information has been published.
Candidates for seats on various Local Governing
Bodies are asked questions—and quite rightly; but
surely it is plain that the League injures the moral force
even of the branches in demanding replies to questions
by the fact that its Executive refused to denounce street
blackguardism committed in its name and refuses to
publish information concerning the voting, etc., on that
matter. If a majority at a meeting of the Executive
vote behind a hedge as it were, upon what ground does
the League stand in asking questions of prospective Poor
Law Guardians and such like? If we wrote to each mem-
ber of the Executive Committee of the League, and asked
—Did you sympathise with or were you afraid to de-
nounce criminal street blackguardism committed in the
name of the League, what would the majority at a recent
meeting say? Butb then the Star Chamber is for passing
sentence ; it is not to be criticised or catechised.

THE STUDY OF HISTORY.

T is a remarkable fact about the English people that

while' they are ready to hold up their hands in horror
at obscurantism, suppression of thought, repression of in-
dividual opinion and the like, when the Catholic Church
is supposed to have been guilty of them through reli-
gious motives, they have no hesitation in doing precisely
these very things through political motives themselves.
The stock argument against a Catholic University is
the limitation of thought it would encourage. I won-
der, then, what would be said about the Catholic Church
if it were to forbid the study of Church History alto-
gether, and lay down that Pope Alexander VI., for in-
stance, was a person never to be mentioned by a Catholic
teacher. Yet this has been exactly the policy of the
English Government in regard to the teaching of Irish
History. No obscurantism charged against the Catho-
lic Church, even if it were true, would have been half
as bad as this political obscurantism, which we have seen
in our own schools. It is, indeed, almost inconceivable
that thinking men should have deemed it in accord with
modern ideas of liberty and progress to banish the his-
tory of Ireland from our national schools during the
whole of the last century. And the action of one so en-
lightened as John Morley in continuing this prohibition
somewhat reminds one of the conduct of the philosophic
Marcus Aurelius in continuing the proscription of
Christianity and the persecution of its professors.

Surely the point of view summed up in the phrase,
“Trish History is for Irishmen to forget’’—and it is
the view of nine-tenths of the conservatives in Ireland
—is the very quintessence of fanatic obscurantism.
It seems to me, indeed, indicative of the most ex-
traordinary mental obliquity. For History is no more
than the story of the past, the sum total of all that has
happened to us, of the good deeds as well as the bad, and

1s pal.

if the logical result of what are said to be the present

good deeds of England, added to her past misdeeds, is
that we should love England and cherish the Union,
which is, of course, the conservative view, why, then
the study of Irish history can but increase that love
and strengthen that cultus. I should hope that no
Irishman would desire untrue history to be taught,
would care to have his children informed that Derry was
taken by storm, that James II. was a Protestant, or Dr.
Troy a patriot; and if the history be true—and history
is not history if it be otherwise—one truth can but con-
firm any other truth, and the study of Irish history but
lend strength to the true view in politics.

Father Dinneen’s lecture on Native History in Na-
tional Schools delivered before the Cumann Taevealac
na Mainceopi N sprunca is but one more example of
that writer’s manifold and invaluable energy. It is
a strong plea for the teaching of Irish history, now that
even a meagre opportunity has at long last been afforded,
and also a discussion of the methods of instruction to be
adopted.  “ Why,”” asks Father Dinneen, “are names
like Aodh O’Neill, Sarsfield, O’Connell, passed over in
silence in the literature which forms the daily mental
food of the children of the Trish race?”’ He aptly com-
pared the child educated in an atmosphere that contains
1o remembrance of the past to the boy brought up in a
“Home '’ with his aspirations sterilised or misdirected.
Nor, indeed, is this vice confined to National Schools,
for the studied contempt and neglect of Trish History in
many Intermediate Schools amounts to a scandal almost

cqually greab. T came across a remarkable example of -

the results of this system quite recently. I was speaking
to a young chap, rather a nice fellow, and good one also,

Oy

TN

ad been educated in one of our best known West
tish Co_llegps. Happening to speak of Patrick’s Day,
St. Patrick is not my patron saint,’” he sneeringly gave

his reason for not observing it. And I may add that
further conversation I found him equally ignorant of
some of the simplest truths of Christianity.

Father Dinneen points out that the teacher must not
merely teach Ivish history as such, but must infuse his
'whole course of instruction with it. Even in teaching
History proper, more depends on the teacher than on the
history, and he must have read much that he may teach
a little.  When dealing with an clementary class he
. should write down such a list of names as the following—
““8t. Patrick, St. Columbkille, Brian Boru, Henry the
Second, Art M‘Morrough, Earl of Desmond, Hugh
“O’Neill, Cromwell, Sarsfield, Swift, O’Connell.”” On
these dozen names as pegs the drapery of Irish History
<could be hung. The names are not all those of Irish-
men, but they are all striking names, and their influence
on Irish history, told vividly and sympathetically,
would make a deep impression on the children. First,
‘the children might commit these names to memory;
then some leading facts about each could be taught;
then further facts; then their influence on their age;
next their inter-connection as links in the chain of his-
toric sequence ; then their chronological position; then
some of the most important contemporary names and
events; and so on. Thus the picture presented to the
R & children will gradually become more distinct and vivid,
and can be embellished with as much colour as the tea-
«cher is able or willing to impart. Instead of taking

names of historical characters the teacher might take
dates or events, and group the history round them, or
‘both systems may be employed together. It appears to
e that in the most elementary class of history which I
- am describing no set text-books need be used except such
a brief skeleton as the teacher himself may draw up and
get stylotyped, or get the children to copy. For more
advanced classes text-books would be necessary, and they
anight study a special period. p A
A really great history of Ireland is, in truth, still
-wanting. ~ Such a history must combine (1) a broad and
philosophic conception of our story as a whole, or, at
Teast, of the period dealt with; (2) a reasonably impar-
tial and scientific treatment; and (3) a brilliant and
popular style. Partiality and prosiness have hitherto
‘been the Scylla and Charybdis of our historians. Lecky,
1in large measure, avoided the former, but he has come
‘hopelessly to grief on the latter. Perhaps Father Din-
‘neen may some day achieve in Irish the task which his-
-torians in the English tongue have failed to perform.
Irish History divides itself into three periods—the free
-period, the story of our first beginnings, and of our later
‘intellectual greatness down to the coming of the Ange-
‘vins; next the unfree Gaelic period down to, the 18th
century, an Iliad of woes, illumined only by the personal
qualities of its heroes; and lastly, the struggles for free-
dom of both English-speakers and Irish-speakers, which
began with the Drapier Letters, and f:or_:tmuc down to
.our day—a period interesting because it is modern, and,
-unlike the preceding one, full of successful achievements,
but clouded in its latter half by commercial and linguis-
itic decay. x

For the treatment of the later periods, we may well
~ -agree with Father Dinneen that we should deal with our

 country’s story in a spirit of scientific (;rutl?, but yet

 with sympathy, as one would deal with one’s mother.

 'The proper treatment of the earlier epoch is more diffi-
.cult.” Of no country is the carly history, wlen treated
as history, entertaining. Who could ever develop any
 keen interest in the fortunes of Edwy or Ethelred? But
-such history may be made interesting when regarded
merely as a collection of legends, IIJI'-ICh as Ll_vy treats
“Roman history, or else when dealt with scientifically as
study of social conditions after the manner of Momm-
_sen. We have had, perhaps, too much of the legendary

course, such a study would only be pursued by more ad-

‘THE MANUFACTURERS AND THE INDUSTRIAL
REVIVAL.

Messrs Paul and Vincent, Chemical Manure Manufac-
turers, Rogerson’s Quay, Britain Quay, and Blackhall
Place, Dublin, write:—

“Dear Sir—Your circular letter of the 15th April
was duly received, but owing to pressure of business was
laid on one side until after the Dublin and Belfast
Spring Shows were past.

“ We are at one with you in the opinion that no solid
business can be established on any other principle than
that of giving the best value which money can buy.
Quality and price must be such as will appeal to the
buyers’ self-interest, and we have always recognised
this fact in our own business.

“If Irish manufacturers wish to secure and retain
the trade of their fellow-countrymen and others, it must
be by giving such value to their buyers as would make
it their interest to continue purchasing.—Yours faith-
fully,

“ Per Pro PAuL & VINCENT,
“W. T. Warson.”

We have received the following letter from Messrs.
John Barrington and Sons, Ltd., 202 Great Britain
Street, the well-known Irish soap manufacturing
firm : —

“ Dear Sir—In reply to your favour of 12th inst., we
beg to answer your questions as follows—1. ‘Do you, asa
manufacturer, wish it to go forth that you desire a
grant-in-aid from your customers and the public?” No.
Certainly not.” 2. ‘Do you claim to meet foreign goods
fairly in the matter of price and quality? ‘Yes.” 3.
‘ Do you sell on merits, or would you wish it to go forth
that as an Irish manufacturer you wanted to unload
your goods as against non-Irish goods of a similar class
on the strength of sentiment involving a pecuniary
sacrifice from your customers? ‘Yes. We sell on our
merits, but we have to be satisfied with a smaller profit
owing to our situation, and the extra cost of coal and
alkali both of which have to be imported. 4. ‘Do you
consider it would injure your industry—even though
you may be giving better value than outside competi-

HOW TO BE HAPPY THOUGH SOBER.

- N attempt to answer this ancient riddle was made
3 in Dublin last week. “ Nothing,” wrote d de-
ceased Irish dramatist, “succeeds like excess,’”’ and the
 latest proof of the truth of that statement was furnished
by the All-Treland Temperance Bazaar. The word
 Temperance *’ has, as we all know, long ago undergone
that change of meaning which frequently is the fato of
words, and nowadays it more often stands for that form
of excess yclept total abstinence than for the moderation
which it should really indicate. Dictionaries define Tom-
perance as habitual moderation in the gratification of the
natural passions, but the individual who sccks to slake
his thirst with alcoholic stimulant, be it never so mode-
rately, will find no welcome in the zecalous band of Tem-
perance Reformers.

In strict accordance with these principles was the féte
at Ballsbridge conducted last week. The bar was closed,
the concert-halls and tea-gardens, the cigar divans dis-
persed, no drink save the cup that cheers, and its twin
sister, coffec; lemonade and ginger beer were the sole
stimulants that warmed the hearts of the gilded youth.

- True, rumour was bruited about now and again that
drink—drink with a capital “D ’’—could be obtained
in some mysterious place, but foew seemed to have actually
discovered this oasis, nor did its guilty guardians adver-
tise its whereabouts. For the vast majority to pass the
turnstile at the entrance of the Royal Dublin Society’s
territory was to take the pledge until they quitted the
grounds again.  Enormous numbers voluntarily took
that pledge. The crowds each night reminded one of
bazaars in the ’nineties, and the scene presented showed
that an answer had been found to the problem embodied
in the words that head this article. No doubt a feeling
pervaded all breasts, Catholic, Protestant, Presbyterian,
and Jew, that it was, at worst, purgatory, and not a
place of eternal thirst.

The bazaar was, in the main, like other bazaars, but it
had distinctive features worthy of recording. Total
abstinence from drink was not its sole display of abnega-
tion; there was, moreover, a total abstinence from
gambling and from politics. The bazaar ticket—that
bane of the visitor who comes actuated by the sentiments
of Mrs. Gilpin—was non-existent, with what result on
the fortunes of the enterprize remains to be seen. Rou-
lette and such like impieties formed no part of the enter-
tainment. For such a state of affairs the aims of the
promoters prepared the public. But that an undertak-
ing purporting to be non-sectarian and non-political
should prove so in reality was, indeed, a matter for sur-
prise. To dwellers in this country the words “ non-
political * have come to convey as distinct a notion of a
particular political party as the words *“ Temperance Re-
formers ”’ suggest advocates of total abstinence. On
this occasion, however, a truce of God was observed.
The flag that braved the baftle and the breeze flaunted
not as of yore. The salvation of a certain monarch was
not nightly prayed for ; the phrase “ Ireland Sober is Ire-
land Free,”” was given a new meaning.

Of course, there were stalls, side-shows, merry-go-
rounds, fireworks, and the customary adjuncts of this
class of money-raising entertainment. Of course, de-
mure damsels attractively attired, vended cigarettes and

‘sweets at a cost out: of all proportion to the value of these
commodities. Of course, young men and maids carcered
inanely round on wooden steeds to the haunting strains
of British music-hall ballads. Barrel-organs, too, vied
with one another in producing pandemonium. Added
to this were the toot-toots of motor-cars, the shrieks of
sirens, and on one occasion the horrors attendant on a
torch-light procession. Nor was the gallantry of Irish
manhood in any way checked by the absence of the rosy
god. In all directions were glimpses to be seen of col-
Ieens and their swains flitting along secluded walks, the
ball-rooms were nightly thronged,-and the entire scene

Gazing on the crowds thronging the Ballsbridge
grounds last week, few could help being struck by one
reflection. Hitherto similar fétes have been organised
for some particular charity. A hospital .h:\s }mua.lly
been the institution seeking to raise funds in this way,
and that it should find supporters among all classes was
no matter for comment. When it is remembered, how-
evor, that the objects of this bazaar last week included
the promoting of legislation for the closing of licensed
premises on Saturday nights and Sundays, and the cur-
tailing of the consumption of alcohol as far as possible,
then is there matter for astonishment in the composi-
tion of the crowds. The “bloods’’ were there. The
“hard nuts,”’ the thirsty men of Dublin, swarmed on all
sides; members of football clubs and similar organisa-
tions which have not as yet unduly compromised them-
selves by their identification with the Temperance Move-
ment, were to be met with at every turn. Nay more,
many were present whose contributions to the cause
were derived from the sale of the evil thing itself, while
some even of this class not alone attended, but actually
assisted at the stalls and booths. After this may we
not hope to see Orangemen attending a bazaar to raise
funds for the United Irish League, or the Victuallers
and Restaurant Proprietors contributing to the coffers
of a Fancy Fair organised in the interests of vege-
tarianism? Yet will not in time to come some thirsty
soul gaze on a Saturday night or Sunday afternoon at
the closed portals and shuttered windows of his
favourite tavern and heave a sigh as he sadly thinks
how the money he spent at the A.I.T.B., has contri-
buted to this dire result?

Yet another distinctive feature must be remembered,
to wit the presence of an Irish Concert Hall. Here
we have another departure from ancient custom. It
is a matter for regret, however, that those in charge
of this particular enterprise were not more fortunate in
the selection of a site. The buzzing of steam-engines
stationed in close proximity, proved an accompaniment
to the songs and recitations too loud to permit of their
being properly heard. The “ get-up ”” of the hall, also,
did not err on the side of comfort, while it must be
admitted that dissertations on the wooings of policemen,
postmen, and domestic servants are not much more ele-
vating than the “coon ’’ songs and music-hall ditties to
be heard in other places. No doubt the entertainment
here provided cannot fairly be judged from one per-
formance, but candour compells the statement that at
least one concert was below the average to be expected—
even at a bazaar.

Seoing that other methods, both overt and covert, were
in vain, a number of Protestant gentlemen put their
heads together in Dublin to devise a more effective pro-
cess of proselytism. No, they would not seek to prose-
lytise the people; they would not wean them from their
old mother tongue. They themselves in fact loved the
old Gaelic (.., in the mouths of Popish peasants), and
they would do their best to preserve it.  But the Chris-
tian love that bound all Christians into one common
family made their hearts melt with pity for the illiteracy
of the people, and they would educate them. Thus arose
the “Irish Society for Promoting the Education of the
Native Irish through the Medium of their own Lan-
guage,’”’ which began operations, I think, in 1818. It
professed simply to teach the people reading and writing
iu the dear old language which they loved, and through
the Word of God which, as Christians, they professed to
love also and thirsted to know. I have now by me
several volumes containing different parts of the Bible
published for that purpose. In proof that their inspira-
tion was charity only the work was maintained by volun-
tary contributions; the people for whom the boon was
meant were not asked to contribute a copper, so unlike
the Popish worship. The British and Foreign Bible
Society had, before the sitting of the Royal Commission
of 1825, spent £80,000 on the printing and publication
of Irish Bibles. Bibles were scattered broadcast every-
where. Dr. Doyle, in a letter * written in 1824, was
able to give this testimony of the Propagandist Zeal : —
“Do not the pawn-offices in every town bear testimony
of the profusion there is of what these saints quaintly

lics call Protestant Bibles—books on which our
peasantry look mnot with vreverence, but with
dread?’” But on the same page he gives the

if you will, that the State exists only for the good of
the people, that we are its subjects, that we pay its
taxes, supply its luxuries, bear all its burdens, fight
and die for its aggrandisement or glory. We will waive
all right to the public money, and sit like Lazarus, ex-

afflict us by interposing your authority between us and

to regard the stranger as entitled to their confidence;
do not intimate to them that their parent and pastor

to beget children in our bondage, and let our name be
 forgotten, and our race extinguished.”” The schools of

THE LEADER.

He proposed our present system of Primary Educa
tion as an improvement on the Kildare Street System,
namely, united secular and separate religious instruc-
tion. He meant well; but the Lord Lieutenant to
whom was left the disposal of the money grant and the
appointment of the Commissioners, gave the Protes-
tants a great preponderance on the Board. In fact,
until Cardinal Cullen became Archbishop of Dublin,
Dr. Whately and Rev. Mr. Carlisle—the former an
English Episcopalian, the Jatter a Scotch Presbyterian—
had completely controlled the administration of the sys-
tem. They had gradually and cunningly brought it
back to the state of the Kildare Street system, when
Cardinal Cullen came on the scene. Archbishop Whately
soon found things getting unpleasant, and after a
year retired because he could no longer with impunity
intrude his proselytising publications into the schools.
For a quarter of a century Cardinal Cullen attacked the
administration’ of the system, and before his death he
had succeeded in drawing the anti-Catholic poison from
the system, and in eliminating from the class-books at
least what was anti-Irish. Had he lived longer he
would have done more.

It is true that parish priests have been managers of
the vast majority of the National Schools. But the
functions of a manager have been restricted from the
beginning to simply seeing that the Rules of the Board
are observed. Managers have never had any control
over the teaching, the subjects taught, or the books
used. Under the Revised Programme of 1901, they
were granted power to initiate programmes suited to
their respective localities. But, as they have been made
to understand more than once, that pretended power to
initiate merely meant the power to suggest, which every
man in the country as well as the managers enjoys with-
out any Revised Programme, or without saying by your
leave to anybody. Whether their initiative would have
been an influence for good or evil on the Primary
Education of the country cannot be known, since they
have never had the right to initiate. A man has no
incentive to put his heart and head into a work unless
he has the power to produce results. Who but an im-
becile would take the trouble to think out programmes
unless he feels that he has an effective voice in their
adoption? Dr. Starkie who, as Resident Commissioner
controls the system, scems to be either incapable or
careless. The Revised Programme, such as it is, has
been carried out in such a hap-hazard way as to leave
one in doubt whether the Resident Commissioner takes
any more pains or gives any more serious thought to
the system which he is highly paid to administer than
to rush occasionally into the Education Office, ovder
men and things about according to his passing humour,
and have his fiat felt in every school in the country. Of
one thing I am certain; he has shown himself blamably
ignorant of the development of the system. I have
read the Address, or rather the attack which he went
to Belfast to deliver against the managers. Whatever
may be the shortcomings of the managers, that address
revealed the Resident Commissioner as a very clumsy
diplomatist and even a crude educationist. He evi-
dently had not grasped some of the leading features of
the English Education system, and whilst he referred
authoritatively to the early Reports of the Irish Com-
missioners, he plainly betrayed the fact that he had not
read them at all. How far the managers would have
shown themselves capable educationists if they had any
initiative power in the schools during the past seventy
years, it 1s hard to say, since they have had no such
power. But considering what the functions of mana-
gers have been in the system, it is neither fair nor in-
genuous to hold them responsible for the character and
morale of the people, so far as these qualities have been
influenced by the teaching of the National Schools.

The managers, it is true, have had the appointment
of the teachers. But the efficiency of teachers depends
largely on their training; yet the National System was
in existence for half a century, its teachers were multi-
plying over the country, and there was only one Train-

with a forty-foot pole.” A few cranks took umbrage

with the exception perhaps of “ A Delegate ”” and a few

lin, at which half-a-dozen Nationalist Members of Par-
liament attended to honour our proceedings and help
our movement. This was done in deference to the feel-
ings of those gentlemen whose colleagues, to the number
of twenty-four were immured in prison at the time, with-
